project.title: N-glycoproteome profiling of rice
project.projectDescription: Metabolic glycan labeling (MGL) is a technique that utilizes bioorthogonal chemical reporters to investigate protein glycosylation. While this method has been widely used for glycoproteomic profiling in animals, its application in N-glycoproteomic research in crops remains limited and requires further development. In this study, we employed N-azidoacetylgalactosamine (GalNAz), a click-compatible sugar, to conduct a large-scale analysis of the N-glycoproteome in rice. Using this approach, we successfully identified 426 N-glycosylation sites and 680 N-glycosylated proteins involved in various important biological processes such as metabolism and stress response. A subset of randomly selected proteins identified by mass spectrometry were validated and confirmed to be N-glycosylated, supporting the reliability of the MGL-based method for N-glycosylation identification in rice. Moreover, by combining mass spectrometry data with biochemical validation, we discovered that the core components of the ERAD-L machinery undergo N-glycosylation. Importantly, this N-glycan modification of the ERAD-L machinery is conserved across both plants and animals. Functional studies revealed the crucial role of N-glycosylation in proper protein degradation within the ERAD-L machinery. Overall, our research significantly expanded the database of N-glycoproteins in rice and presented a highly efficient and practical approach for large-scale identification of N-glycosylated proteins, which holds potential applications for identifying N-glycosylated proteins in other crops.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: For mass spectrometry analysis, 10 mg of labeled and unlabeled protein lysates were separately subjected to a click reaction with alkyne-biotin. The resulting click reaction products were then precipitated using methanol-chloroform to remove any excess click reagents. The resulting pellets from the methanol-chloroform precipitation after the click reaction were re-suspended in 1.5 ml of PBS buffer containing 1.2% SDS. The concentration of SDS was subsequently diluted to 0.2% with PBS, and the samples were filtered through a 0.2-μm filter. Next, 150 μl of streptavidin agarose beads, which had been pre-washed, were added to the protein solution, followed by rotation for 4 hours at room temperature. The beads were then washed sequentially with PBS (pH 7.4) and Milli-Q water five times to remove nonspecifically bound proteins. Subsequently, the beads were re-suspended in 500 μl of 6 M urea containing 10 mM DTT and incubated at 37 °C for more than 60 minutes. Afterward, 20 mM IAA was added and incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 45 minutes. The beads were washed with PBS, followed by centrifugation, and then re-suspended in 200 μl of TEAB buffer containing 1 M urea, 1 mM CaCl2, and 10 ng/μl trypsin. Trypsin digestion was performed on a rotary shaker for more than 18 hours at 37 °C. After digestion, the supernatants were collected, and the beads were washed twice with 100 μl of Milli-Q water. To identify GalNAz-labeled proteins, the supernatants and washing eluents were mixed and subjected to stable isotopic dimethyl labeling as previously described (Boersema et al., 2009). In brief, peptide samples from GalNAz-labeled plants and unlabeled plants (300 μL each) were supplemented with 12 μL of 0.6 M NaBH3CN (Acros) and 12 μL of 4% (vol/vol) 13CD2O (heavy dimethylation labeling) or CH2O (light dimethylation labeling), respectively. The solutions were incubated at room temperature for 1 hour, followed by the addition of 96 μL of 1% (vol/vol) ammonia solution. After adding 48 μL of formic acid, the light and heavy isotopically labeled samples were combined and dried in a rotary evaporator under vacuum (SpeedVac). The peptides were re-dissolved in 90 μL Milli-Q water and subjected to high-pH reverse phase C18 column chromatography (Agela Technologies) using an Agilent HPLC system. Briefly, the peptides were loaded onto the column with buffer A: 2% acetonitrile (ACN) - 98% H2O, pH 10.0, adjusted with NH3H2O, at a flow rate of 1.5 mL/min for 20 minutes. The peptides were separated into 27 fractions using a gradient of 0% B (0 min), 5% B (0-0.1 min), 8% B (0.1-1 min), 14% B (1-7 min), 24% B (7-14 min), 40% B (14-16 min), 95% B (16-18 min), and 15% B (18-20 min). Buffer D consisted of 98% ACN - 2% H2O, pH 10.0, adjusted with NH3H2O. The separation was performed at 45 °C, and the column temperature was controlled by a built-in column heater. The 28 fractions were combined into 12 fractions as follows: fractions 1, 2, and 15; fractions 3, 4, and 16; fractions 5 and 17; fractions 6 and 18; fractions 7 and 19; fractions 8 and 20; fractions 9 and 21; fractions 10 and 22; fractions 11 and 23; fractions 12 and 24; fractions 13 and 25, 26; and fractions 14 and 27, 28. All fractions were dried in a vacuum rotary evaporator (CHRIST) and re-dissolved in 0.1% formic acid (vol/vol) for LC-MS/MS analysis. For the identification of N-glycosylated sites, the supernatants after trypsin digestion were removed, and the beads were washed sequentially with PBS (pH 7.4) and Milli-Q water five times. To release the modified peptides, the beads were eluted with 200 μl of 50 mM ABC and incubated with PNGase F/A for 12 hours at 37 °C. After centrifugation, the eluents were collected, and the beads were washed with 200 μl of Milli-Q water two times. All the eluents were combined and dried in a vacuum rotary evaporator for LC-MS/MS analysis
project.dataProcessingProtocol: The data processing of dimethylated reductive was conducted using Thermo Proteome Discoverer 2.4 software. The Oryza sativa L. proteome database, downloaded from MUS on July 4th, 2017, was used for analysis. Database search was performed with the following parameters: allowing for two trypsin missed cleavage sites, using precursor and fragment mass tolerances of 10 ppm and 0.02 Da, respectively; carbamidomethyl (Cys), dimethyl labeling (N-terminus), and dimethyl labeling (Lys) were chosen as static modifications with tag masses of +28.03130 Da (light) and +34.06312 Da (heavy), respectively. Oxidation (Met, 15.995 Da) was selected as a variable modification. The false discovery rate (FDR) for peptide identification was calculated using the Percolator algorithm in the Proteome Discoverer workflow based on the search results against a decoy database and was set at 1% FDR. The abundances extracted from MS1 spectra of each identified peptide peaks were used to calculate the relative ratio, subsequently. Proteins that appeared in at least two out of three biological replicates and had a minimum of two unique peptides were subjected to further evaluation of enrichment significance. This was performed using an empirical Bayes moderated t-test implemented in the Limma package within R/Bioconductor as described (59). Proteins with a P-value less than or equal to 0.05 and a fold change equal to or greater than 1.5 were reported as GalNAz-modified proteins. The raw data of N-glycosites were processed using the MaxQuant software environment (version 1.6.2.10). The Oryza sativa L. proteome database was employed for both full MS and tandem MS/MS searches, utilizing the Andromeda search engine integrated within the MaxQuant framework. The following parameters were set for the database search: a precursor mass tolerance of 20 ppm for the initial search and 4.5 ppm for the main search, with a fragment mass tolerance of 0.5 Da. Enzyme digestion was performed using trypsin with allowance for up to two missed cleavages, and the minimum peptide length was set to seven amino acids. A false discovery rate (FDR) of 0.01 was applied to filter proteins and peptides. Asp-Asn was considered as a variable modification, while carbamidomethyl was selected as a fixed modification.

references.referenceLine: Lei C, Li X, Li W, Chen Z, Liu S, Cheng B, Hu Y, Song Q, Qiu Y, Zhou Y, Meng X, Yu H, Zhou W, Chen X, Li J. Chemical Glycoproteomic Profiling in Rice Seedlings Reveals N-glycosylation in the ERAD-L Machinery. Mol Cell Proteomics. 2025 24(2):100883
